core_meaning: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> has two nearly opposite meanings depending on context: (1) 'appropriate / suitable' (the positive sense, e.g. the right tool for the job), and (2) 'half-assed / slapdash / done without care' (the negative colloquial sense, which is extremely common in daily speech). Beginners often only learn the first meaning and are confused when native speakers use it to criticize careless work or vague, noncommittal answers.

sentences.jp: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>答<rt>こた</rt></ruby>えといたら、<ruby>先生<rt>せんせい</rt></ruby>にめちゃくちゃ<ruby>怒<rt>おこ</rt></ruby>られた。
sentences.translation: I gave a half-assed answer and the teacher got super mad at me.
sentences.notes: Classic negative sense — <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>に doing something means doing it carelessly or without real thought.

sentences.jp: ねえ、<ruby>夕飯<rt>ゆうはん</rt></ruby><ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>にする？　<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>でいいよ、なんでも。
sentences.translation: Hey, what do you want for dinner? Anything's fine, whatever works.
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> softens into 'whatever is convenient / I'm not fussy' — a very natural use among friends or family.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>には、コミュニケーション<ruby>能力<rt>のうりょく</rt></ruby>が<ruby>高<rt>たか</rt></ruby>い<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>が<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>だと<ruby>思<rt>おも</rt></ruby>います。
sentences.translation: I think someone with strong communication skills would be well-suited for this job.
sentences.notes: The positive, formal-leaning sense: 'suitable / appropriate' — most common in written job descriptions or professional recommendations.

sentences.jp: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>い<ruby>訳<rt>わけ</rt></ruby>ばかりしてるから、<ruby>誰<rt>だれ</rt></ruby>も<ruby>信用<rt>しんよう</rt></ruby>しなくなったんだよ。
sentences.translation: Because you keep making up flimsy excuses, nobody trusts you anymore.
sentences.notes: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>い<ruby>訳<rt>わけ</rt></ruby> ('slapdash excuse') is a very natural collocation showing the negative sense paired with a noun.

sentences.jp: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>時期<rt>じき</rt></ruby>に<ruby>一度<rt>いちど</rt></ruby>ご<ruby>連絡<rt>れんらく</rt></ruby>いただければ<ruby>幸<rt>さいわ</rt></ruby>いです。
sentences.translation: We would appreciate it if you could contact us at a convenient time.
sentences.notes: The positive sense in formal/written Japanese — <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>時期<rt>じき</rt></ruby> means 'a suitable / appropriate time,' with no negative nuance at all.

dont_use: Don't assume context will make the meaning clear — <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>にやっといて said by a boss can mean either 'use your judgment' or come across as 'just slap something together,' which can cause real miscommunication. In formal written Japanese where you mean 'appropriate,' consider using <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> instead to avoid any ambiguity, since <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> has become so strongly associated with the careless meaning in spoken registers. Also avoid using <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> to compliment someone's work — it may sound like you're calling it sloppy.

confused_with.contrast: A common learner mistake is writing <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>対応<rt>たいおう</rt></ruby> in a formal report when criticizing poor handling of a situation — but this is ambiguous and may read as 'appropriate response' rather than 'careless response.' <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> means strictly 'appropriate / fitting' with no negative sense, so it's the safer choice in writing and formal speech: <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby>な<ruby>判断<rt>はんだん</rt></ruby> ('sound judgment') is unambiguous, while <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>判断<rt>はんだん</rt></ruby> could raise eyebrows.

frequency: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> is extremely common in daily spoken Japanese, especially in the negative 'half-assed' sense. The positive 'suitable' sense appears frequently in formal writing, job postings, and official documents. You'll hear the casual sense constantly among friends, at work, and in manga/anime. Overall one of the more useful and frequently misunderstood words at this level.
anki_hint: Remember: in <ruby>会話<rt>かいわ</rt></ruby>, <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> almost always means 'sloppy / half-assed / vague on purpose' — save the 'suitable' meaning for formal/written contexts, or swap in <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> to be safe.
